case_name: Diazinon removal from groundwater for municipal drinking water production
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for diazinon-contaminated groundwater intended for drinking water supply under moderate cost and conventional operation constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A small town operates groundwater wells influenced by nearby agricultural land and has detected diazinon in the raw water. The utility needs a reliable treatment approach to produce drinking water using established, practical technologies that can be run by operators with conventional treatment experience. The source water is otherwise relatively clean groundwater with low turbidity and low natural organic matter, so the main design focus is removal of the pesticide while maintaining stable finished water quality.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve robust reduction of diazinon to a level suitable for drinking water supply and maintain consistent potable water quality.
secondary_goals: keep operation straightforward for a small municipal utility
maintain reasonable media or residuals handling requirements

core_unit_functions.prefiltration: remove incidental particles and protect downstream adsorber performance
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb diazinon and provide the primary barrier for dissolved pesticide removal
core_unit_functions.post_disinfection: ensure microbiological safety in finished drinking water

applicability_rationale: granular activated carbon is a well-established drinking water treatment option for hydrophobic trace organic compounds including many pesticides
the groundwater has low turbidity and low DOC, which supports effective GAC adsorption and longer bed life
the treatment train is compatible with conventional municipal operation and does not require unusually high energy input
for a single dissolved organic contaminant in otherwise clean groundwater, GAC is a practical and strongly supported mainstream choice
potential_risks: GAC breakthrough can occur if empty bed contact time and media replacement frequency are not properly managed
higher than expected DOC or co-contaminant loading could shorten carbon life
spent carbon handling and reactivation logistics must be planned

core_unit_functions.prefiltration: provide basic particle control and stabilize downstream treatment
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_contact: adsorb diazinon during chemical contact period
core_unit_functions.filtration: remove PAC-associated solids and polish water quality

applicability_rationale: PAC is a conventional adsorptive option for organic micropollutant control where a flexible chemical dosing approach is desired
the approach can be implemented using familiar chemical feed and filtration practices at utilities with conventional treatment capability
it can serve as a feasible alternative where fixed-bed GAC installation is less attractive or where seasonal adjustment is useful
potential_risks: PAC performance depends on contact time, dose control, and effective solids separation
continuous solids generation increases sludge handling requirements compared with fixed-bed GAC
removal may be less stable than GAC if dosing is not well optimized

core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation: oxidize diazinon and transform it into more biodegradable or more readily polishable compounds
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_or_gac_filtration: remove oxidation byproducts and provide adsorption or biological polishing

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation processes are technically capable of degrading many pesticides and can be effective when adsorption alone is insufficient or when additional safety margin is desired
pairing oxidation with downstream carbon or biologically active filtration is important to capture transformation products
this is a credible but somewhat more complex option than direct activated carbon treatment for a straightforward groundwater case
potential_risks: incomplete oxidation may generate intermediate byproducts that require downstream polishing
process control for oxidant dose, UV intensity, or peroxide feed is more demanding than basic adsorption systems
additional monitoring may be needed to verify byproduct management

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide physical rejection of dissolved contaminants including many organic micropollutants
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust treated water chemistry after membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.post_disinfection: ensure microbiological safety and distribution system protection

applicability_rationale: pressure membranes can reduce dissolved pesticides and provide a strong multi-contaminant barrier
the train is technically feasible for diazinon removal but is not the most natural fit when the source water is otherwise relatively clean and only one organic contaminant is the primary issue
membranes are usually considered when broader salinity or multiple dissolved contaminant control is also needed
potential_risks: concentrate management can be difficult for small groundwater systems
membrane fouling control and integrity monitoring add operational burden
the process may be unnecessarily intensive relative to the treatment objective

core_unit_functions.air_stripping: attempt removal of volatile compounds by phase transfer to air

applicability_rationale: included as a low-ranked comparator because it is a familiar groundwater treatment concept in some contaminant settings
however diazinon is not a strong candidate for volatilization-based removal, so the mechanism is poorly matched to the contaminant
potential_risks: ineffective removal is the main risk because diazinon is not sufficiently volatile for air stripping to be a preferred treatment mechanism
ranking this option too highly would ignore contaminant-specific treatability
could create false confidence if selected without bench validation

preferred_rank_pattern: activated_carbon_based_processes should rank at or near the top for diazinon in relatively clean groundwater
advanced_oxidation may appear as a technically feasible but lower-ranked alternative to direct carbon adsorption
high_pressure_membranes should not rank first under moderate cost and conventional operation constraints

must_reflect: preference for proven drinking water technologies for pesticide removal
moderate operational capability of a small municipal groundwater utility
avoidance of unnecessarily complex or high-energy systems when adsorption can address the contaminant

must_contain: why activated carbon is appropriate for hydrophobic organic pesticide removal
why low turbidity and low DOC groundwater supports adsorption-based treatment
why advanced oxidation needs downstream polishing and greater process control
why membrane options rank lower under the stated constraints

should_mention: carbon breakthrough and media replacement planning
competition from natural organic matter or other organics for adsorption sites
possible oxidation byproducts when advanced oxidation is used
concentrate disposal burden for membrane treatment
